title: Why Do Used Cars Cost So Much Less Than New Ones?

challenges.stem: You're hiring for a senior role. A highly qualified candidate is available immediately — no notice period, no counteroffer from current employer. Your recruiter says 'red flag — if they were that good, they'd be retained.' Is this reasoning valid?

options.text: It's the lemons problem in action — availability is being read as a negative signal. But it could equally mean layoffs, relocation, or a bad manager. Ask for the information directly rather than inferring from the meta-signal.
Yes, the recruiter is right — top performers are always retained by their current employers
No, availability has nothing to do with quality and the recruiter is being prejudiced
You should hire them quickly before someone else does — availability is a feature, not a bug

challenges.explanation: This IS the lemons problem — availability is read as a negative signal ('if good, why available?'). But the inference isn't automatically correct. The solution isn't to ignore the signal (option C) or blindly accept it (option B) or rush (option D). It's to resolve the information asymmetry: ask directly about circumstances, verify references, propose a trial period. Make private information visible.

challenges.stem: In Akerlof's used car market, good-car owners withdraw rather than accept below-value offers. This makes the remaining pool worse, which makes buyers offer even less. What force drives this death spiral?

options.text: Trust — the absence of verifiable information makes buyers distrust ALL sellers equally, punishing good sellers and rewarding bad ones
Scarcity — as good cars leave the market, the remaining cars become scarcer and prices should rise
Fear — buyers are irrationally afraid of being cheated
Incentives — sellers are incentivized to lie about car quality

challenges.explanation: The core force is broken trust from information asymmetry. Buyers can't verify quality, so they distrust uniformly. This isn't irrational fear (option C) — it's rational given the information gap. Scarcity (option B) would raise prices only if quality were distinguishable. And while sellers could lie (option D), the problem exists even with honest sellers — because buyers can't verify honesty. The fix is making information visible, restoring trust.

pairs.back: When one party in a transaction knows more than the other — the seller knows the car's history, the employee knows their real performance, the company knows its real culture
The spiral where information gaps cause good options to withdraw and bad options to dominate — 'the willing seller is probably a lemon'
Akerlof's insight: when buyers can't distinguish quality, they offer average prices, driving good sellers out until only 'lemons' remain
Solve the lemons problem by making private information (seller quality) public through accumulated, verifiable transaction history

pairs.right: Seller transfers risk to themselves — only possible with good products (costly signal of quality)
Makes employer quality visible to candidates — fixes the 'why so many open positions?' suspicion

pairs.left: Health insurance premiums rising until healthy people drop out
pairs.right: Adverse selection: sickest people buy most, driving costs up, driving healthy out
Third-party inspection creates information parity between buyer and seller

pairs.left: Dating app where most persistent users are least successful
pairs.right: Adverse selection in relationship markets — heavy usage becomes a negative signal

before.label: Trusting or suspecting
before.text: I either trust the other party's claims (risky) or I'm suspicious of everything (miss good deals). In a world of hidden information, I just have to guess and hope.

after.label: Designing for information parity
after.text: I don't guess — I build or use mechanisms that make hidden information visible. Warranties, trial periods, reputation systems, verifiable history. The question isn't 'do I trust them?' but 'what structure makes their private information observable?'
